author: Clement of Rome
interpretation: Clement appeals to the creation narrative as evidence of God's orderly character. He reads the sequential days as a model for the ordered harmony God desires in the church, drawing a direct line from cosmic order to ecclesial order. Creation demonstrates that God assigns each element its proper place and role.
context: Writing to a divided Corinthian church, Clement uses creation as a template for proper authority structures and peaceful submission to appointed leaders.

author: Theophilus of Antioch
interpretation: Theophilus provides one of the earliest extended commentaries on the creation days, reading them as literal sequential periods. He argues that the three days before the luminaries were created demonstrate that light itself is independent of the sun, originating directly from God. He sees the creation week as a typological pattern pointing to the ages of human history.
context: Writing to a pagan interlocutor named Autolycus, Theophilus defends the superiority of the Genesis account over Greek cosmogonies and mythological origins.

author: Origen of Alexandria
interpretation: Origen argues that the creation days cannot be taken literally since there are 'days' before the sun exists. He reads the account allegorically: the six days represent stages of spiritual ascent, and the seventh day foreshadows eschatological rest. The physical creation narrative encodes deeper truths about the soul's journey toward God.
context: Origen pioneered the Alexandrian allegorical method, influencing centuries of Eastern Christian exegesis. His approach was controversial even in his lifetime and was later partially condemned at the Fifth Ecumenical Council.

author: Basil of Caesarea
interpretation: Basil insists on the plain meaning of the creation days, directly opposing Origen's allegorizing. He argues that 'day' means a literal day and that the text describes real physical processes. Water, light, vegetation — each emerged in the order described. Basil also emphasizes that creation ex nihilo distinguishes the biblical God from all philosophical cosmologies.
context: The Hexaemeron is a series of nine homilies delivered during Lent. Basil explicitly rejects allegorical readings of Genesis 1, making it a foundational text for literal-historical interpretation in the Eastern church.

author: Augustine of Hippo
interpretation: Augustine argues that creation was instantaneous rather than sequential — God created all things simultaneously, and the six days represent a logical rather than temporal ordering, structured for angelic understanding. The 'days' are categories of angelic knowledge about creation, not periods of time. He warns Christians against making scientifically ignorant claims about Genesis that would embarrass the faith before educated pagans.
context: Despite the title 'literal commentary,' Augustine's reading is far from modern literalism. His instantaneous creation view influenced medieval theology and his caution about science-faith conflicts remains widely cited.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas mediates between Augustine's simultaneous creation and Basil's sequential days, considering both defensible. He focuses on the philosophical implications: creation ex nihilo establishes God as the sole uncaused cause, and the ordered progression of days demonstrates divine wisdom in giving each creature its proper nature and teleological purpose.
context: Aquinas treats the creation account within his broader framework of the Five Ways and divine causation. His willingness to hold multiple patristic readings in tension became a model for Catholic exegetical humility.

author: Hugh of St. Victor
interpretation: Hugh insists that the literal-historical sense of Scripture must be mastered before any allegorical or moral reading is attempted. For Genesis 1, this means taking the sequential creation days seriously as the foundation upon which spiritual meanings are built. He compares those who skip the literal sense to builders who neglect a foundation.
context: Hugh's emphasis on the literal sense as foundational — not exclusive but primary — helped recover historical exegesis within the medieval school of St. Victor and influenced later Reformation hermeneutics.

author: Martin Luther
interpretation: Luther takes the six days as literal twenty-four-hour periods and explicitly rejects Augustine's instantaneous creation as well as Origen's allegory. He argues that the plain words of Scripture should be trusted even when they seem impossible to human reason. The creation account reveals God as a God who speaks things into existence — the same word-power at work in the Gospel.
context: Luther's Genesis lectures, delivered over his final decade, represent his most sustained exegetical work. His insistence on the plain sense here exemplifies the Reformation principle of sola scriptura applied to the most fundamental text.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin reads the creation days as literal but emphasizes divine accommodation: God described creation in terms ordinary people could understand, not in the technical language of astronomers. The account is therefore historically true but pedagogically shaped. Calvin focuses on the theological purpose — God created an ordered world as a theater for displaying divine glory.
context: Calvin's concept of accommodation became enormously influential. It allowed later Reformed thinkers to accept scientific findings about the cosmos without viewing them as contradicting Scripture's intent.

author: Julius Wellhausen
source_title: Prolegomena to the History of Israel
interpretation: Wellhausen assigns Genesis 1:1–2:3 to the Priestly source (P), dating it to the exilic or post-exilic period. He reads the structured seven-day framework as reflecting priestly concerns with Sabbath observance and cultic order. The account is not primitive cosmology but a late, sophisticated theological composition designed to ground Israel's worship calendar in the structure of creation itself.
context: The Documentary Hypothesis revolutionized biblical studies by identifying multiple literary sources within the Pentateuch. Wellhausen's dating of P as late remains debated but his source analysis continues to shape academic discussion of Genesis.

author: Gerhard von Rad
interpretation: Von Rad reads Genesis 1 as a doxological confession rather than a scientific report. The Priestly author shaped the creation account as a liturgical proclamation of God's sovereign ordering of chaos. Each creative act follows a structured formula — command, execution, evaluation, naming — that reflects worship patterns. The text answers the question of who created, not how.
context: Von Rad's form-critical approach treated Genesis 1 as kerygmatic (proclamatory) theology rather than historical reportage. His commentary remains one of the most influential on Genesis in the twentieth century.

author: John H. Walton
source_title: The Lost World of Genesis One
interpretation: Walton argues that Genesis 1 describes functional origins rather than material origins. In the ancient Near Eastern cognitive environment, to 'create' meant to assign function and purpose, not to manufacture material substance. The seven days describe God inaugurating the cosmos as a cosmic temple, with day seven as God taking up residence. This reading removes the perceived conflict with modern science entirely.
context: Walton's functional ontology proposal generated significant evangelical debate. It allows an ancient reading of the text that neither requires concordism with modern science nor dismisses the historicity of the account.

author: G.K. Beale
source_title: The Temple and the Church's Mission
interpretation: Beale develops the cosmos-as-temple theme extensively. He argues that the Garden of Eden was the first sacred space — the Holy of Holies — and that the creation account establishes a pattern where God's presence fills progressively larger sacred spaces. The tabernacle and temple consciously recapitulate creation symbolism, and the new creation in Revelation completes the pattern.
context: Beale's work on cosmic temple theology has become foundational for biblical-theological readings of creation. His approach integrates Genesis 1 into the whole-Bible narrative of God's expanding presence.
